summary: My family has a long history in Nassau County, Florida, dating back to 1792 when we received a Spanish land grant. Our plantation, New Hope, is located there, and it was our home before the Civil War forced us to relocate temporarily. Nassau County holds many memories of my childhood and family life before the war disrupted everything.

Fernandina Beach, Florida, was the town that grew out of a plantation owned by Don Domingo Fernández, who set aside land for it. My family lived there before the Civil War, and it was where I spent much of my youth. After the war, we returned to Fernandina, though the house was stripped of its furniture and piano during our absence.

My daughter worked as a stenographer at the Windsor Hotel, which was part of our daily life. It was a place connected to her public work, and it helped support our family after my son’s tragic death. The hotel represents a part of the community where my family was involved professionally.

New Hope is the name of our plantation in Nassau County, Florida. It was a place I wished to visit again, and my son left me a V-8 automobile that could take us there. New Hope symbolizes the family’s roots and the land we cherished before the war.

My mother and her family, the Gunbys, were educated in St. Marys, Georgia, and it was where my parents were married in 1830. I attended school there under Mr. Charah, and it was an important place for our early education. St. Marys was a significant part of our family history and schooling.

Don Domingo Fernández was the original owner of the plantation that later became Fernandina Beach. He set aside land for the town, which was named after his family. His legacy shaped the community where my family lived and grew up.

Stephen Fernández married my mother’s sister, Eliza Gunby, and they had four children. When his wife died, he entrusted his children to my parents to raise alongside their own eleven children. Stephen was part of the extended family that shaped my upbringing.

Eliza Gunby was my mother’s sister and the wife of Stephen Fernández. After her death, her children came to live with my parents. She was part of the Gunby family, who were well educated and influential in our early life.

My brother James was fortunate enough to attend Harvard University after receiving early education at home. His time there was part of the broader educational opportunities our family sought for the children. Harvard represented the pinnacle of academic achievement in our family.

My brother John attended college in Rochester, New York, during the Civil War, which made his return home difficult due to the conflict. He had to take a long and indirect route back to Fernandina for safety. Rochester was part of the northern education my family valued despite the war.

My sister Judy, who later became Mrs. Bacon, went to a girls’ school in Milledgeville, Georgia. This was part of the varied education my siblings received across different states. Milledgeville was important in shaping my sister’s early education.

My sister Mary attended college in Athens, Georgia, where she furthered her education. Athens was one of the places where our family sent children to gain advanced schooling. It was part of the educational journey for my siblings.

My sister Florence attended the Troy Female Seminary in New York, where Miss Frances Willard was principal. This school was renowned as one of the finest girls’ schools in the country and was ahead of its time, especially in promoting women’s rights. Florence’s education there was a source of pride for our family.

Frances Willard was the principal of the Troy Female Seminary when my sister Florence attended. She later became famous in the Women’s Christian Temperance Union and was a pioneer for women’s rights. Her leadership influenced the education my sister received.

The Women’s Christian Temperance Union was an organization that Frances Willard, my sister’s school principal, became famous for leading. It was a movement ahead of its time advocating for women’s rights and temperance. Though I was not directly involved, it influenced the education and values my sister Florence received.

The American Civil War uprooted our family life in Fernandina, forcing us to flee to Hamilton County, Florida, near White Springs. During the war, my brothers served in the Confederate Army, and education became a challenge as schools closed. We lived through hardship but maintained schooling at home, and after the war, we returned to find our home stripped of its furnishings.

During the Civil War, my family moved to Middle Florida, specifically Hamilton County near White Springs, to escape the conflict. We lived there in safety and plenty, away from the turmoil in Fernandina. It was a place of refuge during those difficult years.

Hamilton County, Florida, near White Springs, was where my family took refuge during the Civil War. We lived there unmolested and set up a school for the children, as there were no formal schools in the area. It was a crucial place for our survival and education during the war.

White Springs, Florida, near where we lived in Hamilton County, was part of our safe haven during the Civil War. It was a quiet place where we could continue our lives and education despite the war raging elsewhere. White Springs represents the peace we found during those troubling times.

Colonel Cooper’s son, John, was one of the boys who boarded with us for school before the war. He was part of the community of families connected through education and social ties. I remember him as a neighbor and part of our extended school family.

Walter Ossinhman, the son of the Slocum family, was another boy who boarded with us for schooling. He was part of the group of boys from neighboring families who studied together under our tutors. Walter was a familiar face in our home and school life.

General Finegan’s son, Woodward, was among the boys who boarded with us for school. After the war, my sister Florence opened a school for young ladies in General Finegan’s old home. The Finegan family was part of our community and its history.

The Ogilvie family, who kept the drawbridge at Amelia River, had sons who boarded with us for school. Their children were part of the extended group of boys educated alongside my siblings. The Ogilvies were an important local family connected to our educational circle.

A tutor sent by Lord & Taylor to teach my brothers was a cousin of Abraham Lincoln. He stayed with us until the late 1800s before returning to New York due to homesickness. This connection to Lincoln’s family was a remarkable part of our educational history.

Lord & Taylor in New York was the firm my father contacted to secure a tutor for his sons. They sent Mr. Lincoln, a cousin of Abraham Lincoln, to teach us. The firm also supplied our family with sheet music and helped preserve it by binding volumes for us.

My brother Isador served in the artillery and took part in the Battle of Olustee during the Civil War. This battle was a significant event in Florida’s war history and part of my family’s military involvement. Olustee represents the sacrifices my family made during the conflict.

My brother Colibar served with the Army of Northern Virginia, Lee’s troops, during the Civil War. His service was part of the family’s commitment to the Confederate cause. The Army of Northern Virginia was a major force in the war and shaped much of our family’s experience.

Several members of my family, including my brothers and relatives, served in the Confederate States Army during the Civil War. My brother John was a Colonel, Charles worked in the Commissariat, and others served in various capacities. Our family’s involvement was deep and personal.

entity: Episcopal Church in the United States of America
summary: After the war, my sister Florence opened a school for young ladies in the old home of General Finegan, which was connected to the Episcopal Church in the United States of America. The church’s bishop recognized the importance of education and supported our efforts. The Episcopal Church played a role in our community rebuilding.

Beaufort, South Carolina, was mentioned as part of the broader region connected to our family’s history and the Civil War. While not a direct home, it was part of the southern landscape during the conflict. It contextualizes the environment in which our family lived.

Abbeville, South Carolina, was another southern locale relevant to the Civil War era and the communities connected to my family. It was part of the regional backdrop during those turbulent times. Though not a direct residence, it was part of the cultural and historical setting.

Suwannee, Tennessee, was mentioned in the context of the broader southern states during the Civil War period. It was part of the network of places connected to families and military movements. It helps frame the geographical scope of our experiences.

Jenny Lind was a famous singer whose repertoire was part of the sheet music collection my family treasured. We had volumes composed entirely of her music, sent from Lord & Taylor in New York. Her music was a delight in our home and part of our cultural life.
